title: Your first test
instruction:
In C# tests, use [Fact] for a single test case.

`Assert.Equal` checks two values are equal.

Run this.
successMessage: AAA pattern: Arrange sets up data, Act performs the operation, Assert verifies the result. In real projects you'd use [Fact] with xUnit.

title: Multiple test cases
instruction:
In xUnit, `[Theory]` with `[InlineData]` runs a test with multiple input sets.

Run this.
successMessage: Testing multiple input/expected pairs systematically covers edge cases. [Theory] + [InlineData] in xUnit does exactly this.

title: Testing exceptions
instruction:
Verify that code throws the right exception.

Run this.
successMessage: In xUnit, Assert.Throws<T>(() => ...) verifies exception type. Testing exceptions is essential for error-handling code.

title: Assert methods
successMessage: xUnit has Assert.Equal, Assert.Contains, Assert.Null, Assert.InRange, Assert.True, Assert.False. Each catches a different type of failure.

title: Arrange-Act-Assert pattern
instruction:
Structure tests clearly with AAA.

Run this.
successMessage: AAA makes the test's intent clear. Anyone reading the test instantly understands what is being set up, what action is taken, and what is verified.

title: Test-driven development
instruction:
Write the test first, watch it fail, then implement.

Run this.
successMessage: TDD: tests document requirements. FizzBuzz is the classic starter — the tests define exactly what the function must do.

title: Parameterised testing with loops
instruction:
Test many edge cases systematically.

Run this.
successMessage: Testing boundary conditions (longer than width, exact width, shorter) ensures the function handles all cases. Edge cases catch most bugs.

title: Testing private logic via public interface
instruction:
Test through the public interface, not by accessing private fields.

Run this.
successMessage: Test through public properties and methods. Private implementation can change without breaking tests — that is the point of encapsulation.
